statement: FRCP Rule 4(c)(2) states that any person who is at least 18 years old and not a party may serve a summons and complaint in federal court.
source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: Any person who is at least 18 years old and not a party may serve a summons and complaint.

statement: FRCP Rule 4(c)(3) provides that at the plaintiff's request, the court may order service by a United States marshal, deputy marshal, or a person specially appointed by the court. The court must so order if the plaintiff is authorized to proceed in forma pauperis under 28 U.S.C. §1915 or as a seaman under 28 U.S.C. §1916.
source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: At the plaintiff's request, the court may order that service be made by a United States marshal or deputy marshal or by a person specially appointed by the court. The court must so order if the plaintiff is authorized to proceed in forma pauperis under 28 U.S.C. §1915 or as a seaman under 28 U.S.C. §1916.

statement: FRCP Rule 4(e) provides that an individual within a judicial district of the United States may be served by: (1) delivering a copy of the summons and complaint to the individual personally; (2) leaving a copy at the individual's dwelling or usual place of abode with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there; or (3) delivering a copy to an agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process.
source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: An individual within a judicial district of the United States may be served in the following ways: (1) by delivering a copy of the summons and of the complaint to the individual personally; (2) by leaving a copy of each at the individual's dwelling house or usual place of abode with some person of suitable age and discretion then residing therein; or (3) by delivering a copy of each to an agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process.

statement: FRCP Rule 4(h) requires service on a domestic or foreign corporation, partnership, or unincorporated association by delivering a copy to an officer, managing or general agent, or any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process, or in the manner prescribed by Rule 4(e)(1) for serving an individual.
source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: Unless federal law provides otherwise or the defendant's waiver has been filed, a domestic or foreign corporation, or a partnership or other unincorporated association that is subject to suit under a common name, must be served: (1) in a judicial district of the United States: (A) in the manner prescribed by Rule 4(e)(1) for serving an individual; or (B) by delivering a copy of the summons and of the complaint to an officer, a managing or general agent, or any other agent authorized by appointment or by law to receive service of process

statement: FRCP Rule 4(m) requires that service of a defendant be completed within 90 days after the complaint is filed. If not, the court must dismiss the action without prejudice or order service within a specified time, unless the plaintiff shows good cause for failure.
source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: If a defendant is not served within 90 days after the complaint is filed, the court—on motion or on its own after notice to the plaintiff—must dismiss the action without prejudice against that defendant or order that service be made within a specified time. But if the plaintiff shows good cause for the failure, the court must extend the time for service for an appropriate period.

statement: FRCP Rule 4(l)(1) requires proof of service by the server's affidavit, except for service by a United States marshal or deputy marshal.
source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: Unless service is waived, proof of service must be made to the court. Except for service by a United States marshal or deputy marshal, proof must be by the server's affidavit.

statement: FRCP Rule 4(d) allows a plaintiff to request that a defendant waive formal service of a summons. If the defendant fails to waive service without good cause, the court must impose on the defendant the expenses later incurred in making service.
source_title: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: An individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service under Rule 4(e), (f), or (h) has a duty to avoid unnecessary expenses of serving the summons. The plaintiff may notify such a defendant that an action has been commenced and request that the defendant waive service of a summons.

statement: Oklahoma adopted the Uniform Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act (UIDDA) on April 23, 2021, when Governor J. Kevin Stitt signed HB 2229 into law. The Act went into effect on November 1, 2021.
source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
quote: On April 23, 2021, Gov. J. Kevin Stitt signed HB 2229 into law. Commonly known as the Uniform Deposition and Discovery Act (UIDDA), this new provision of Oklahoma law went into effect Nov. 1, 2021, codified in 12 O.S. §3250, et seq., (OSCN 2022).

statement: Oklahoma's UIDDA is codified at 12 O.S. §§ 3250 through 3257, under Chapter 41B - Uniform Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act of 2021.
source_title: Title 12 of the Oklahoma Statutes - OSCN
quote: Chapter 41B - Uniform Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act of 2021: § 3250. Short Title; § 3251. Definitions; § 3252. Subpoena - Procedure; § 3253. Subpoena - Service; § 3254. Subpoena - Response; § 3255. Application for Protective Order, or to Enforce, Quash or Modify Subpoena; § 3256. Uniformity of the Law Among the States; § 3257. Discovery Requests in Cases Pending on November 1, 2021

statement: Under 12 O.S. § 3252, a party seeking to serve an out-of-state subpoena in Oklahoma must submit a foreign subpoena to a clerk of court in the Oklahoma county in which discovery is sought. The clerk shall promptly issue a subpoena for service upon the person to which the foreign subpoena is directed.
source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
quote: Section 12 O.S. §3252 sets forth the procedure whereby a foreign subpoena is honored in Oklahoma. Specifically, 'a party must submit a foreign subpoena to a clerk of court in the county in which discovery is sought' in Oklahoma. The UIDDA provides that the clerk, upon receipt of the foreign subpoena, 'in accordance with that court's procedure, shall promptly issue a subpoena for service upon the person to which the foreign subpoena is directed.'

statement: The Oklahoma subpoena issued under UIDDA must incorporate the terms used in the foreign subpoena and contain or be accompanied by the names, addresses, and telephone numbers of all counsel of record and any party not represented by counsel. A request by a foreign attorney for issuance of an Oklahoma subpoena pursuant to the UIDDA does not constitute an appearance in the courts of Oklahoma.
source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
quote: Section 12 O.S. §3252(C)(1-2) provides the Oklahoma subpoena must 'incorporate the terms used in the foreign subpoena; and contain or be accompanied by the names, addresses and telephone numbers of all counsel of record in the proceeding with which the subpoena related and of any party not represented by counsel.' Notably, a request by a foreign attorney for issuance of an Oklahoma subpoena pursuant to the UIDDA 'does not constitute an appearance in the courts of [Oklahoma].'

statement: Under 12 O.S. § 3253, a subpoena issued under the UIDDA must be served in accordance with 12 O.S. § 2004.1, and the duties of a person served are governed by 12 O.S. § 2004.1(D).
source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
quote: Once an Oklahoma subpoena is issued containing provisions consistent with those found in the foreign subpoena, 12 O.S. §3253 states the Oklahoma subpoena must be served in accordance with 12 O.S. §2004.1. With respect to the duties of a person in Oklahoma served with such a subpoena, the UIDDA again refers us to 12 O.S. §2004.1, this time expressly incorporating the language found in 12 O.S. §2004.1(D).

statement: Under 12 O.S. § 3255, a party may apply for a protective order or to enforce, quash, or modify an issued subpoena. Such applications must be submitted in the county in which discovery is to be conducted and are governed by the rules or statutes of Oklahoma.
source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
quote: Section 3255 makes clear that litigation regarding the subpoena is governed by the 'rules or statutes of this state,' and requests to enforce, quash or modify the subpoena must be 'submitted in the county in which discovery is to be conducted.'

statement: To serve an Oklahoma subpoena in another state, an Oklahoma attorney should locate the version of the UIDDA adopted in the target state, contact the court clerk in the county where the subpoena will be served, submit the Oklahoma subpoena with any required fee, and the clerk will issue a local subpoena conforming to the Oklahoma subpoena, which is then served pursuant to the foreign jurisdiction's procedures.
source_title: Out-of-State Discovery Simplified: How the Interstate Depositions and Discovery Act Can Save You and Your Client Time and Money - Oklahoma Bar Association
quote: Typically, the court clerk will require a letter invoking the local version of the UIDDA and the payment of a small fee for issuance of the foreign subpoena. Once the clerk receives your duly issued Oklahoma subpoena, they should then issue a local subpoena conforming to the Oklahoma subpoena. The local subpoena will then be transmitted back to counsel for service pursuant to the foreign jurisdiction's procedures for service of subpoenas.

statement: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) is codified at 50 U.S.C. §§ 3901-4043 and applies to all civil proceedings, including state court proceedings, to protect servicemembers on active duty.
source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: This section applies to any civil action or proceeding, including any child custody proceeding, in which the defendant does not make an appearance.

statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(b)(1) requires that before a default judgment may be entered against a defendant who does not appear, the plaintiff must file an affidavit stating whether or not the defendant is in military service and showing necessary facts in support of the affidavit.
source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: (1) Affidavit requirement. If a defendant does not make an appearance in an action described in subsection (a), the court may not enter a default judgment against the defendant until the plaintiff files an affidavit-- (A) stating whether or not the defendant is in military service and showing necessary facts in support of the affidavit; or (B) if the plaintiff is unable to determine whether the defendant is in military service, stating that the plaintiff is unable to determine whether the defendant is in military service.

statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(b)(2) provides that if a court determines that a defendant is in military service, the court may not enter a judgment until after the court appoints an attorney to represent the defendant.
source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: If the court determines that the defendant is in military service, the court may not enter a judgment until after the court appoints an attorney to represent the defendant.

statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(b)(3) allows the court to require the plaintiff to post a bond as a condition of entry of a default judgment if the court is unable to determine whether the defendant is in military service. The bond may be used to indemnify the defendant if later found to be a servicemember.
source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: If the court is unable to determine whether the defendant is in military service, the court may-- (A) require the plaintiff to file a bond in an amount approved by the court to indemnify the defendant against any loss or damage the defendant may suffer by reason of any judgment entered against the defendant, should the defendant later prove to have been in military service at the time the judgment was entered; and (B) make such other disposition as may be equitable.

statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(d) requires a court to grant a stay of proceedings for a minimum period of 90 days when a defendant in military service has not appeared, if the court determines there may be a defense that cannot be presented without the defendant's presence, or if appointed counsel cannot contact the defendant after due diligence.
source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: In an action covered by this section in which the defendant is in military service, the court shall grant a stay of proceedings for a minimum period of 90 days under this subsection upon application of counsel, or on the court's own motion, if the court determines that-- (1) there may be a defense to the action and a defense cannot be presented without the presence of the defendant; or (2) after due diligence, counsel has been unable to contact the defendant or otherwise determine if a meritorious defense exists.

statement: 50 U.S.C. § 3931(g) allows a servicemember to have a default judgment entered during active duty (or within 60 days after termination) reopened upon application filed not later than 90 days after termination or release from military service, if the servicemember was prejudiced by military service in making a defense and has a meritorious defense.
source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: If a default judgment is entered in an action covered by this section against a servicemember during the servicemember's period of military service (or within 60 days after termination of or release from such military service), the court entering the judgment shall, upon application by or on behalf of the servicemember, reopen the judgment... if it appears that-- (A) the servicemember was materially affected by reason of that military service in making a defense to the action; and (B) the servicemember has a meritorious or legal defense to the action or some part of it.

statement: The SCRA affidavit requirement may be satisfied by a statement, declaration, verification, or certificate in writing subscribed and certified or declared to be true under penalty of perjury. Knowingly filing a false affidavit carries criminal penalties of fine or imprisonment for not more than one year, or both.
source_title: 50 U.S. Code § 3931 - Protection of servicemembers against default judgments | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: The requirement for an affidavit under paragraph (1) may be satisfied by a statement, declaration, verification, or certificate, in writing, subscribed and certified or declared to be true under penalty of perjury. (c) A person who makes or uses an affidavit permitted under subsection (b) knowing it to be false, shall be fined as provided in title 18, or imprisoned for not more than one year, or both.

statement: Military service status under the SCRA can be verified through the Defense Manpower Data Center (DMDC) website at https://scra.dmdc.osd.mil. A Single Record Request can be submitted to obtain a report certifying Title 10 active duty status.
source_title: SCRA - Servicemembers Civil Relief Act | DMDC
quote: Users may submit a Single Record Request to obtain a report certifying Title 10 active duty status for provisions under SCRA. An account is required to use Single Record Request.

statement: The DMDC SCRA website requires the individual's last name and Social Security number for verification. If the Social Security number is not available, a manual search can be requested by mail to: Defense Manpower Data Center, Attn: Military Verification, 1600 Wilson Blvd., Suite 400, Arlington, VA 22209-2593.
source_title: Plaintiff's Guide to SCRA Compliance - Maryland Courts
quote: If the plaintiff has the last name and social security number of the defendant, the plaintiff may access the Defense Manpower Data Center web site at https://www.scra.dmdc.osd.mil The plaintiff must enter the defendant's last name and social security number. If a Social Security number is not available, verification may also be made by contacting the DOD at: Defense Manpower Data Center, Attn: Military Verification, 1600 Wilson Blvd. Suite 400, Arlington, Virginia 22209-2593.

statement: The SCRA covers members of the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard on active duty; members of the Reserves on federal active duty; members of the National Guard called to active duty for more than 30 days under federal orders; and commissioned officers of the Public Health Service and National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
source_title: Servicemembers' Civil Relief Act (SCRA) | United States Courts
quote: The Act applies to members of the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Coast Guard on active duty as defined in 10 U.S.C. 101(d)(1). The Act may also apply to members of the National Guard, and commissioned members of the Public Health Service and the National Oceanographic and Atmospheric Administration.

statement: The Military Lending Act (MLA) is a separate statute from the SCRA that protects active duty servicemembers and their dependents at the point of credit origination, capping interest and fees at 36% Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR). The MLA uses the same DMDC verification system as the SCRA.
source_title: SCRA and MLA: What is the Difference? | Experian
quote: The Servicemember Civil Relief Act (SCRA) protects service members and their dependents (indirectly) on existing debts when the service member becomes active duty. In contrast, the Military Lending Act (MLA) protects service members, their spouses and/or covered dependents at point of origination if they are on active duty at that time. The MLA limits interest rates and fees to 36 percent Military Annual Percentage Rate (MAPR).

statement: The Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA) is codified at 15 U.S.C. §§ 1692-1692p and applies to the collection of debts incurred by consumers primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.
source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
quote: This subchapter may be cited as the 'Fair Debt Collection Practices Act.' 15 U.S.C. 1692a: The term 'debt' means any obligation or alleged obligation of a consumer to pay money arising out of a transaction in which the money, property, insurance or services which are the subject of the transaction are primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(a)(1) prohibits debt collectors from communicating with consumers at unusual times, defined as before 8:00 a.m. and after 9:00 p.m. local time at the consumer's location, unless the consumer consents.
source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692c - Communication in connection with debt collection | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: Without the prior consent of the consumer given directly to the debt collector or the express permission of a court of competent jurisdiction, a debt collector may not communicate with a consumer in connection with the collection of any debt-- (1) at any unusual time or place or a time or place known or which should be known to be inconvenient to the consumer. In the absence of knowledge of circumstances to the contrary, a debt collector shall assume that the convenient time for communicating with a consumer is after 8 o'clock antemeridian and before 9 o'clock postmeridian, local time at the consumer's location.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(a)(2) prohibits a debt collector from communicating with a consumer who is represented by an attorney, if the debt collector knows or can readily ascertain the attorney's name and address, unless the attorney fails to respond or consents to direct communication.
source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692c - Communication in connection with debt collection | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: if the debt collector knows the consumer is represented by an attorney with respect to such debt and has knowledge of, or can readily ascertain, such attorney's name and address, unless the attorney fails to respond within a reasonable period of time to a communication from the debt collector or unless the attorney consents to direct communication with the consumer.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(b) prohibits debt collectors from communicating with third parties about a debt without the consumer's prior consent or court permission, except as reasonably necessary to effectuate a postjudgment judicial remedy or as permitted under section 1692b (location information).
source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692c - Communication in connection with debt collection | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: Except as provided in section 1692b of this title, without the prior consent of the consumer given directly to the debt collector, or the express permission of a court of competent jurisdiction, or as reasonably necessary to effectuate a postjudgment judicial remedy, a debt collector may not communicate, in connection with the collection of any debt, with any person other than the consumer, his attorney, a consumer reporting agency if otherwise permitted by law, the creditor, the attorney of the creditor, or the attorney of the debt collector.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692c(c) allows a consumer to stop all communication from a debt collector by sending a written request. Upon receipt, the debt collector must cease further communication except to advise of termination of efforts, notify of remedies, or notify of intent to invoke a specified remedy.
source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692c - Communication in connection with debt collection | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: If a consumer notifies a debt collector in writing that the consumer refuses to pay a debt or that the consumer wishes the debt collector to cease further communication with the consumer, the debt collector shall not communicate further with the consumer with respect to such debt, except-- (1) to advise the consumer that the debt collector's further efforts are being terminated; (2) to notify the consumer that the debt collector or creditor may invoke specified remedies which are ordinarily invoked by such debt collector or creditor; or (3) where applicable, to notify the consumer that the debt collector or creditor intends to invoke a specified remedy.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692d prohibits harassment or abuse in debt collection, including the use or threat of violence, obscene or profane language, publication of lists of consumers who refuse to pay debts, and repeatedly or continuously causing a telephone to ring with intent to annoy.
source_title: 15 U.S. Code § 1692d - Harassment or abuse | LII / Cornell Law School
quote: A debt collector may not engage in any conduct the natural consequence of which is to harass, oppress, or abuse any person in connection with the collection of a debt. Without limiting the general application of the foregoing, the following conduct is a violation of this section: (1) The use or threat of use of violence or other criminal means... (2) The use of obscene or profane language... (5) Causing a telephone to ring or engaging any person in telephone conversation repeatedly or continuously with intent to annoy, abuse, or harass any person at the called number.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692e prohibits false, deceptive, or misleading representations in connection with debt collection, including misrepresenting the amount of a debt, falsely implying legal action will be taken, using false representation of the legal status of any debt, and using any false representation or deceptive means to collect a debt.
source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
quote: A debt collector may not use any false, deceptive, or misleading representation or means in connection with the collection of any debt. Without limiting the general application of the foregoing, the following conduct is a violation of this section: (2)(A) The false representation of the character, amount, or legal status of any debt... (10) The use of any false representation or deceptive means to collect or attempt to collect any debt or to obtain information concerning a consumer.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692f prohibits unfair practices by debt collectors, including collecting amounts not expressly authorized, accepting postdated checks by more than five days without written notice, depositing postdated checks prior to the date shown, taking nonjudicial action to dispossess property without a present right, and communicating by postcard.
source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
quote: A debt collector may not use unfair or unconscionable means to collect or attempt to collect any debt. Without limiting the general application of the foregoing, the following conduct is a violation of this section: (1) The collection of any amount (including any interest, fee, charge, or expense incidental to the principal obligation) unless such amount is expressly authorized by the agreement creating the debt or permitted by law. (4) Depositing or threatening to deposit any postdated check or other postdated payment instrument prior to the date on such check or instrument. (7) Communicating with a consumer regarding a debt by post card.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692g requires a debt collector to provide a written validation notice within five days of the initial communication, including: the amount of the debt; the name of the creditor; a statement that the consumer has 30 days to dispute the debt; and notice that upon written dispute, the collector will obtain verification or a copy of any judgment.
source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
quote: Within five days after the initial communication with a consumer in connection with the collection of any debt, a debt collector shall, unless the following information is contained in the initial communication or the consumer has paid the debt, send the consumer a written notice containing-- (1) the amount of the debt; (2) the name of the creditor to whom the debt is owed; (3) a statement that unless the consumer, within thirty days after receipt of the notice, disputes the validity of the debt, or any portion thereof, the debt will be assumed to be valid by the debt collector; (4) a statement that if the consumer notifies the debt collector in writing within the thirty-day period that the debt, or any portion thereof, is disputed, the debt collector will obtain verification of the debt or a copy of a judgment against the consumer and a copy of such verification or judgment will be mailed to the consumer by the debt collector.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692g(b) provides that if the consumer disputes the debt in writing within 30 days, the debt collector must cease collection efforts until verification or a copy of the judgment is mailed to the consumer.
source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
quote: If the consumer notifies the debt collector in writing within the thirty-day period described in subsection (a) that the debt, or any portion thereof, is disputed, or that the consumer requests the name and address of the original creditor, the debt collector shall cease collection of the debt, or any disputed portion thereof, until the debt collector obtains verification of the debt or a copy of a judgment, or the name and address of the original creditor, and a copy of such verification or judgment, or name and address of the original creditor, is mailed to the consumer by the debt collector.

statement: The FDCPA defines 'debt collector' to include any person who regularly collects debts owed to another, but excludes officers or employees of a creditor collecting debts in the creditor's name, and creditors collecting their own debts under their own name.
source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
quote: The term 'debt collector' means any person who uses any instrumentality of interstate commerce or the mails in any business the principal purpose of which is the collection of any debts, or who regularly collects or attempts to collect, directly or indirectly, debts owed or due or asserted to be owed or due another. The term does not include-- (A) any officer or employee of a creditor while, in the name of the creditor, collecting debts for such creditor.

statement: CFPB Regulation F (12 CFR Part 1006), effective November 30, 2021, implements the FDCPA and provides additional requirements including: a presumption of compliance for call frequency (no more than 7 calls within 7 days to a particular consumer); expanded requirements for electronic communications; and a model validation notice form with safe harbor protections.
source_title: Bona Fide Error Defense for Final Debt Collection Rule | Smith Debnam
quote: In 2020 and 2021, the CFPB amended Regulation F. The amendments, which address a broader range of topics, such as debt collection communications, and restate the substantive provisions of the FDCPA, became effective on November 30, 2021. Section 1006.14 establishes a bright line by placing numeric limitations on the placing of telephone calls... Section 1006.34 of the Rule reinvents the Debt Validation Notice by requiring significantly more robust disclosures.

statement: Oklahoma does not have state-specific debt collection laws that regulate third-party debt collectors beyond the federal FDCPA. Oklahoma residents are protected solely by the federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act. However, the Oklahoma Consumer Protection Act (Okla. Stat. tit. 15, §§ 751-763) provides broader consumer protections.
source_title: Debt Collection Laws in Oklahoma: Know Your Rights | Upsolve
quote: Oklahoma doesn't have any state-specific laws that regulate third-party debt collectors or original creditors. But residents are still fully protected by the federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA).

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692k provides for civil liability for FDCPA violations, including actual damages, additional statutory damages up to $1,000 per violation, attorney's fees, and costs. In class actions, statutory damages are capped at the lesser of $500,000 or 1% of the debt collector's net worth.
source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
quote: Any debt collector who fails to comply with any provision of this subchapter with respect to any person is liable to such person in an amount equal to the sum of-- (1) any actual damage sustained by such person as a result of such failure; (2)(A) in the case of any action by an individual, such additional damages as the court may allow, but not exceeding $1,000; or (B) in the case of a class action, such amount for each named plaintiff as could be recovered under subparagraph (A), and such amount as the court may allow for all other class members, without regard to a minimum individual recovery, not to exceed the lesser of $500,000 or 1 per centum of the net worth of the debt collector.

statement: 15 U.S.C. § 1692n provides that the FDCPA does not annul, alter, or affect any state law except to the extent that such state law is inconsistent with the FDCPA, and then only to the extent of the inconsistency.
source_title: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act - Federal Trade Commission
quote: This subchapter does not annul, alter, or affect, or exempt any person subject to the provisions of this subchapter from complying with the laws of any State with respect to debt collection practices, except to the extent that those laws are inconsistent with any provision of this subchapter, and then only to the extent of the inconsistency.
